revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:本瀧寺]]
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Hontaki-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Myōken-shū|qid=Q11447017|1=Myōken-shū}} |image = File:Hontakiji hondo.jpg | location = 718 Noma-naka, Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.935278|N|135.467778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}} | founded = 1921 | founder = Noma Nisshō | country = Japan | website = [http://www.hontakiji.com/ Official Website] | prefecture= {{ill|Nose|qid=Q1203349|1=Nose, Osaka}} }}
'''Hontaki-ji''' (本瀧寺) is the head temple of the [[Myōken-shū]] sect of Buddhism, located in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|en|Nose, Osaka|qid=Q1203349|lt=Nose Town|1=Nose, Osaka}}, [[Toyono District]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, Japan. The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is "Nose Myōkenzan" (能勢妙見山).
Situated on the slopes of [[Myōkenzan (Osaka and Hyōgo)|Mount Myōken]], Hontaki-ji is often confused with the {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|en|Nichiren Buddhism|qid=Q871371|lt=Nichiren-sect|1=Nichiren Buddhism}} temple at the mountain's summit, [[Shinnyo-ji|Shinnyo-ji Temple's Noseda Myōken-dō]], but the two are entirely unrelated. Hontaki-ji is a [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai]]-based temple associated with [[Shugendō]] practices.
== History == Hontaki-ji was founded in {{ill|1921|en|1921|qid=Q2162|lt=1921|1=1921}} (Taishō 10) by Noma Nisshō, a Tendai Shugendō monk, at an ancient ascetic training site known as Hontaki. Establishing a new temple during this era required significant bureaucratic approval, so the temple was formally established under the relocated name of a Tendai temple in Nara called Kunkō-in.
In {{ill|1926|en|1926|qid=Q5259|lt=1926|1=1926}} (Taishō 15/Shōwa 1), the temple was renamed "Hontaki-ji." After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, in {{ill|1946|en|1946|qid=Q18610|lt=1946|1=1946}} (Shōwa 21), the temple became independent from the [[Tendai sect]], founding the [[Myōken-shū]] sect of Buddhism and establishing Hontaki-ji as its head temple.
; Principal Deities : The main object of worship is [[:en:Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha]], flanked by the auxiliary deities {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Daibosatsu|1=Myōken}} (Sanskrit: Sudrsti) and [[:en:Uka-no-Mitama|Tsunetomi Daibosatsu]].
; The Hontaki of Nose : In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, the waterfall located on the northern slopes of Mount Myōken was opened by {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|qid=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} as a training site for water asceticism and became known as "Gyōgi Falls" (later "Hontaki"). Noma Nisshō constructed a temple at the former site of Tsunetomi Hall, one of the halls belonging to Nose Myōken-dō, establishing the foundation for the current Hontaki-ji.
: The temple’s name derives from the nearby waterfall, located southeast of the temple grounds. Historical evidence suggests the waterfall was called "Myōken Waterfall" or "Myōkenzan Waterfall" before the Edo period. The falls, featuring a large stone torii at the entrance, are surrounded by numerous stone Buddhist statues along the paths connecting Hontaki-ji to the mountain summit. Figures such as the [[:en:Tenrikyō|founder of Tenrikyō]], {{ill|Nakayama Miki|en|Nakayama Miki|qid=Q2723199|lt=Nakayama Miki|1=Nakayama Miki}}, and {{ill|Honmon Butsuryū-shū|en|Honmon Butsuryū-shū|qid=Q5890962|lt=Honmon Butsuryū-shū|1=Honmon Butsuryū-shū}} founder [[Nagamatsu Seifū]] are said to have trained at Hontaki.
== Motorcycles and Hontaki-ji == The temple has gained fame as a pilgrimage site for motorcycle enthusiasts. This reputation was sparked by the vice abbot brothers, who are avid {{ill|Harley-Davidson|en|Harley-Davidson|qid=Q192814|lt=Harley-Davidson|1=Harley-Davidson}} riders. Motorcycle riders visit the temple for blessings and protective prayers, and the temple distributes "Motorcycle Temple" stickers and guardian bells to its visitors. On weekends and Mondays, a café operates on the temple grounds, providing a scenic rest stop for both riders and pilgrims.
== References == * ''Hontaki-ji Engi'' (History of Hontaki-ji), Myōken-shū Sōmubunkyoku, 1997. * ''Tōgō-mura-shi'' (History of Tōgō Village), 1916. * ''Nose Monogatari,'' 1836. * ''Nose Tōgō-shi,'' 1823. * ''Settsu Meisho Zue'' (Famous Sites of Settsu Province), 1796.
== Related Articles == * [[Nose Myōken-dō]] * {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}} * [[Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyōgo)]] * [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhism]]
== External Links == * [http://www.hontakiji.com/ Official Website of Hontaki-ji] * [https://ja-jp.facebook.com/%E3%83%90%E3%82%A4%E3%82%AF%E5%AF%BA-%E6%9C%AC%E7%80%A7%E5%AF%BA-324922784732904/?ref=page_internal/ Hontaki-ji Facebook Page] * [https://instagram.com/hontakiterrace?igshid=YmMyMTA2M2Y= Hontaki-ji Terrace Instagram]
